title: What temperature should each room be?
description: Recommended temperatures for every UK room, from 16 to 18°C bedrooms to 21°C living rooms, plus what each extra degree adds to your heating bill.
blurb: Living rooms, bedrooms, bathrooms and spare rooms all want different temperatures. Here is the room-by-room guide.
lede: Most UK homes run on one thermostat set to one number, which means the living room is about right, the bedrooms are too warm for good sleep, and the spare room is quietly growing mould behind the wardrobe. Different rooms have different jobs, and they want different temperatures. Getting each one right is more comfortable than blasting the whole house to 21°C, and it is cheaper too, because every degree you shave off a room you are not sitting in cuts real kilowatt hours off the bill.
short_answer: Aim for 18 to 21°C in living rooms, 16 to 18°C in bedrooms, and no lower than about 14°C in rooms you rarely use, so condensation and mould cannot take hold. The NHS advises a minimum of 18°C for anyone elderly, very young or unwell. Each degree you trim cuts roughly 10 per cent off heating fuel use, worth around £73 a year for a typical gas-heated home at current prices.

sections.html:
These figures draw on NHS cold weather guidance, World Health Organization research on healthy indoor temperatures, and Energy Saving Trust advice. They are targets for when the room is in use. Empty rooms can sit several degrees lower, with a floor of about 14°C to keep damp away.

RoomRecommended temperatureWhy
Living room19 to 21°CYou sit still here for hours, so it needs the warmest setting in the house
Bedroom (adults)16 to 18°CYour body needs to drop its core temperature to fall asleep; a cool room helps
Baby's room16 to 20°CLullaby Trust safer-sleep guidance; overheating is a known risk for infants
Bathroom20 to 22°C while in useYou are wet and undressed, so it feels colder than the thermometer says
Kitchen17 to 19°CThe oven, hob and your own activity add free heat while you cook
Home office19 to 21°CSitting at a desk is as sedentary as the sofa, so treat it like a living room
Hallway and landing15 to 18°CYou pass through rather than linger; it just needs to take the edge off
Spare and unused rooms14 to 16°CLow enough to save money, high enough to keep condensation off cold walls

Based on NHS, World Health Organization, Energy Saving Trust and Lullaby Trust guidance, checked June 2026.

One important exception: if anyone in the household is over 65, under 5, or managing a health condition such as COPD, asthma or heart disease, the NHS advice is to keep the rooms they use at 18°C or above around the clock. The savings from running cooler are not worth the health risk for vulnerable people.

sections.h2: Why bedrooms should be cooler than living rooms
sections.html:
The single biggest mistake in most homes is heating bedrooms to living room temperatures. Your body has to lower its core temperature by about a degree to initiate sleep, and a bedroom in the 16 to 18°C range helps that process along. Sleep researchers consistently find that people fall asleep faster and wake less often in a cool room under a warm duvet than in a warm room under a thin one.

The duvet does the work the radiator would otherwise do, and it does it for free. A 10.5 tog duvet in a 17°C room is comfortable for most adults; swap to 13.5 tog if you genuinely feel cold rather than turning the radiator up. If the room still feels chilly when you get into bed, an electric blanket run for 20 minutes before lights out costs a fraction of a penny and beats heating the whole room all evening.

Babies are the exception in both directions

Infants cannot regulate temperature the way adults can, so their rooms should not run as cold as an adult bedroom, but overheating is the bigger documented danger. The Lullaby Trust recommends 16 to 20°C with a correctly rated sleeping bag and no extra quilts or pillows. A simple room thermometer in the nursery is a few pounds well spent.

If your heating runs on a single hallway thermostat, the bedrooms upstairs are probably tracking whatever the hall does. The fix is at the radiator, not the thermostat, and we cover that below.

The rule of thumb, backed by Energy Saving Trust modelling, is that each 1°C reduction in heating temperature cuts space heating fuel use by roughly 10 per cent. A typical three-bedroom gas-heated home burns in the region of 10,000 kWh a year on space heating alone, on top of hot water and cooking. That makes the cost of running the whole house warmer easy to estimate.

Whole-house heating levelApprox. annual space heating useApprox. annual cost
18°C8,000 kWh£586
19°C9,000 kWh£660
20°C10,000 kWh£733
21°C11,000 kWh£806
22°C12,000 kWh£880

At 7.33p per kWh for gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Based on a typical home using around 10,000 kWh a year for space heating at 20°C; your figures will vary with insulation, size and weather.

So each degree across the whole house is worth roughly £73 a year at current gas prices, and more in a larger or draughtier home. The trick is that you do not need to choose one number for everything. Run the living room at 20°C and let the bedrooms, hall and spare room sit several degrees lower, and you capture most of that saving without ever feeling it. Our thermostat settings guide covers the whole-house number in more depth, and the wider heating section has the full set of room-heating cost guides.

sections.h2: How to give each room its own temperature
sections.html:
A standard UK setup has one room thermostat, usually in the hall, plus thermostatic radiator valves (TRVs) on most radiators. That combination can already deliver per-room temperatures; most people just never adjust the valves.

The thermostat sets the ceiling for the whole system. The TRV on each radiator then throttles that room individually, closing the valve once its local target is reached. The numbers on a TRV map only roughly to temperature, and it varies by brand, but as a starting point on a typical valve:

  • Setting 2: around 15 to 16°C, right for halls, landings and spare rooms
  • Setting 3: around 19 to 20°C, right for living rooms and offices
  • Between 2 and 3: around 17 to 18°C, right for bedrooms
  • Frost setting (the snowflake): around 5 to 7°C, only for rooms you have genuinely shut down

Set them once, then leave them alone for a week and judge by a cheap room thermometer rather than by feel. Two practical warnings. First, never fit a TRV set low in the room where the wall thermostat lives, because the radiator will shut off before the thermostat is satisfied and the boiler will run forever trying to close the gap; leave that radiator's valve fully open. Second, if some rooms heat fast and others crawl, the system likely needs balancing and proper zoning before TRV tweaks will behave predictably.

Smart TRVs add per-room schedules, so a child's bedroom can warm up for bedtime while yours stays cool, but the £25 to £60 per radiator cost only pays back if you will actually use the schedules. Whatever the valves are doing, also check your boiler flow temperature: a condensing boiler set to 60°C flow or lower runs noticeably more efficiently, and lower-temperature running suits a house where most rooms are deliberately set cool.

The spare room, turned off completely

Shutting a radiator off entirely feels thrifty, but an unheated room in a UK winter can drop below 12°C, and its walls colder still. Warm moist air from the rest of the house drifts in, hits those cold surfaces, and condenses. The result is mould behind furniture and on external walls by February. Keep unused rooms at 14°C or so, keep the door closed, and crack the window briefly now and then. If you are weighing up exactly how far to cut back, see our guide to turning radiators off in unused rooms, and if black spots have already appeared, the condensation and mould fixes guide covers cheap remedies.

The bathroom, heated all day for two showers

The bathroom only needs to be warm while someone is wet in it. A towel rail or radiator on a timer or smart TRV for an hour around the morning rush does the job; holding 22°C all day in the most humid room of the house is pure waste.

The hallway, used as the benchmark

The wall thermostat usually lives in the hall, which is typically the coldest space on the ground floor thanks to the front door. If you set it to 21°C to make the hall feel warm, the living room may be cruising at 23°C. Set the thermostat by how the rooms you live in feel, not by how the hallway feels, and fix the front door draughts separately.

The kitchen, heated like a lounge

An hour of oven and hob use dumps a couple of kilowatt hours of heat into the kitchen for free. Set its TRV low and let cooking top it up.

One temperature for the whole house is the expensive way to be only partly comfortable. Set the living spaces to 19 to 21°C, drop bedrooms to 16 to 18°C, hold rarely used rooms at 14°C or so to keep damp out, and let the kitchen and hallway run cool. The tools to do this, a room thermostat plus TRVs, are already fitted in most UK homes and cost nothing to adjust.

The prize is real: each degree trimmed across the house saves roughly 10 per cent of space heating fuel, about £73 a year on gas at the current cap for a typical home, and a cooler bedroom will improve your sleep into the bargain. The only firm exception is health. Anyone elderly, very young or unwell needs their rooms at 18°C minimum, whatever the bill says.

faq.q: Is 18°C too cold for a bedroom?
faq.a: No. For healthy adults, 16 to 18°C is the range sleep researchers recommend, because your body needs to shed heat to fall asleep. A warm duvet does the rest. The exception is anyone elderly, under 5 or with a health condition, where the NHS advises keeping rooms at 18°C or above.

faq.q: What temperature stops damp and mould in unused rooms?
faq.a: Keep the room at about 14°C or higher, keep the door closed so moist air from the rest of the house stays out, and ventilate briefly every few days. Mould takes hold when warm humid air condenses on cold wall surfaces, so the danger zone is an unheated, unventilated room with the door left open.

faq.q: What temperature should a baby's room be?
faq.a: The Lullaby Trust recommends 16 to 20°C, with the baby in a correctly rated sleeping bag and no loose bedding. Overheating is a bigger documented risk for infants than mild cold, so resist the urge to run the nursery warmer than this. A simple room thermometer costs a few pounds.

faq.q: Should I just turn radiators off in rooms I never use?
faq.a: Turn them down, not off. A fully unheated room in winter invites condensation and mould on cold external walls, and it leaches heat from the rooms next to it. A TRV on setting 1 to 2, holding around 14 to 15°C, saves most of the money with none of the damp risk.

faq.q: Why does my living room feel cold even at 20 or 21°C?
faq.a: Usually it is cold surfaces and moving air rather than air temperature. Large windows, uninsulated walls and draughts make you lose heat by radiation and draught chill even when the thermometer reads 21°C. Heavier curtains, draught-proofing and radiator balancing typically fix it more cheaply than turning the thermostat up further.
